title: DAVID — Ancient Greek: Restoring the Pitch Accent

gate_0.music_plan: original score only — music cleared, cue sheet on file

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Ancient Greek — the language of Homer, Plato, and the New Testament, read for millennia — but rarely with the pitch accent its poets counted.
Modern classrooms teach Erasmian or modern Greek habits. The corpus itself carries accent marks, meter, and ancient commentators who argued about how words rose and fell.
Can we recover a living contour of speech from dots and circumflexes that medieval scribes still bothered to write?
Homeric meter, Dionysius Thrax on accent, and papyri with acute and circumflex marks give structural proof. Byzantine scholia preserve debates about quantity and stress shift.
We test reconstructed pitch accent against tragic trimeter and Homeric hexameter, cross-check Koine papyri against classical norms, and cite where Roman-era speakers already pronounced differently.
Accent notation in surviving manuscripts is attested as written tradition. Exact peak pitch and regional Koine color are reconstructed — I mark both before the line is spoken.
Listen — ἄνδρα μοι ἔννεπε, μοῦσα — Sing to me, Muse, of the man — Homeric opening with restored pitch accent and long vowel quantity.
Follow the Archive — and hear Greek as meter demands, not as modern habit allows.
